title: Psalms 96
subtitle: Sing to the LORD a New Song

sections.header: Verses 1–6 — “Declare His Glory Among the Nations”

heb.paragraph: "Sing to the LORD a new song (shîr ḥādāsh); sing to the LORD, all the earth" (v.1). Three times "sing to the LORD" in two verses. Proclaim his salvation daily (v.2). Declare his glory among the nations (v.3). The gods of the nations are idols (ʾĕlîlîm), but the LORD made the heavens (v.5). Splendour and majesty before him; strength and glory in his sanctuary (v.6). Parallel to 1 Chronicles 16:23–33.

notes.note: "Sing to the LORD a new song. Proclaim his salvation day after day. Declare his glory among the nations." Daily proclamation, global declaration. Worship is missionary.

"Great is the LORD and most worthy of praise; he is to be feared above all gods. For all the gods of the nations are idols, but the LORD made the heavens." The comparison: idols vs Creator. No contest.

refs.note: This psalm appears nearly verbatim in David's song when the ark entered Jerusalem.
The new song sung to the Lamb echoes the psalm's call for new songs to YHWH.
The 144,000 singing a new song before the throne fulfills the psalm's worship vision.

hist.context: Psalm 96 calls all earth to sing a 'new song' — not merely chronologically new but qualitatively fresh, matching God's new acts of salvation. The missionary scope — 'declare his glory among the nations' (v.3) — extends beyond Israel. The polemic against idols who 'made' nothing contrasts with YHWH who made the heavens.

heb.paragraph: "He will judge the world in righteousness (bəṣedeq) and the peoples in his faithfulness" (v.13). The call goes cosmic: let the heavens rejoice, let the earth be glad (v.11). Let the sea resound, the fields be jubilant, the trees of the forest sing (vv.11–12). Creation worships because the Judge is coming. "He comes to judge the earth" (v.13). Judgment is not feared but celebrated — because this Judge is righteous and faithful.

notes.note: "Say among the nations, ‘The LORD reigns.’ Let the heavens rejoice, let the earth be glad, let the sea resound, let the fields be jubilant, let all the trees of the forest sing for joy." The most comprehensive worship summons in the Psalter: heaven, earth, sea, fields, forests — all join.

"He comes to judge the earth. He will judge the world in righteousness and the peoples in his faithfulness." Judgment is good news. When the Judge is righteous, his coming produces celebration, not terror.

refs.note: Worshiping created things rather than the Creator indicts what the psalm mocks as idol-worship.
The living God who made heaven and earth contrasts with worthless idols, echoing the psalm.
Creation rejoicing in God's presence fulfills the psalm's vision of nature celebrating the coming Judge.

hist.context: The creation's response — heavens rejoicing, earth glad, sea resounding, fields jubilant, trees singing (vv.11-12) — personifies nature awaiting divine judgment. The coming Judge will 'judge the world in righteousness' (v.13). Unlike human fear of judgment, creation welcomes divine rule as setting things right.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: God is a key figure in Sing to the LORD a New Song.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVFor all the gods of the nations are idols, but the Lord made the heavens.ESVFor all the gods of the peoples are worthless idols, but the LORD made the heavens.NIVWorship the Lord in the splendor of his holiness; tremble before him, all the earth.ESVWorship the LORD in the splendor of holiness; tremble before him, all the earth!

rec.text: ""Sing to the LORD a new song. Proclaim his salvation day after day. Declare his glory among the nations." Daily proclamation, global declaration. Worship is missionary."

rows.label: New Song — 96:1–6
rows.text: Three times "sing." Declare among the nations. "All gods are idols; the LORD made the heavens."

hebtext.note: "Sing to the LORD a new song (shîr ḥādāsh); sing to the LORD, all the earth" (v.1). Three times "sing to the LORD" in two verses. Proclaim his salvation daily (v.2). Declare his glory among the nations (v.3). The gods of the nations are idols (ʾĕlîlîm), but the LORD made the heavens (v.5). Splendour and majesty before him; strength and glory in his sanctuary (v.6). Parallel to 1 Chronicles 16:23–33.

"He will judge the world in righteousness (bəṣedeq) and the peoples in his faithfulness" (v.13). The call goes cosmic: let the heavens rejoice, let the earth be glad (v.11). Let the sea resound, the fields be jubilant, the trees of the forest sing (vv.11–12). Creation worships because the Judge is coming. "He comes to judge the earth" (v.13). Judgment is not feared but celebrated — because this Judge is righteous and faithful.

themes.note: Worship peaks at 10: all creation sings. Mission at 8: "declare among the nations." Prophecy: the coming Judge is righteous.

prayer_prompt: The call to sing a new song declares God's glory among the nations. Consider asking God what new song He's putting in your heart — what fresh praise He deserves.
